check: Semantic HTML elements over generic divs
description: Use button, nav, main, article, label elements for their actual purpose rather than styling a div to look like one
why_it_matters: Screen readers and keyboard navigation rely on semantic meaning, not visual appearance — a div styled as a button gets none of the built-in keyboard/focus/announcement behavior for free

check: Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text)
description: Verify text-against-background contrast ratios meet the minimum, especially for secondary/muted text which commonly fails this
why_it_matters: Insufficient contrast makes text unreadable for users with low vision, and is one of the most common and easily automatable-to-detect accessibility failures

check: Every interactive element reachable and operable by keyboard alone
description: Tab through the entire interface without a mouse — every button, link, form control, and custom widget must be focusable and actionable via keyboard
why_it_matters: Keyboard-only users (motor impairments, power users, screen reader users) are entirely blocked by any interactive element that's mouse-only

check: Visible focus indicator on every focusable element
description: Never remove outline or focus styles without providing an equally visible custom replacement
why_it_matters: Without a visible focus indicator, a keyboard user has no way to tell which element is currently active — this is a common and easily-avoidable regression from 'clean up the outline' CSS resets

check: Images have meaningful alt text (or empty alt for decorative images)
description: Every image has an alt attribute — descriptive for meaningful images, empty (not missing) for purely decorative ones
why_it_matters: Missing alt text means screen reader users get no information about an image's content; a missing (not empty) alt attribute can also cause the filename to be announced instead, which is worse than nothing

check: Form inputs have associated, visible labels
description: Every input has a label pointing to it or wrapping it — not a placeholder alone, which disappears on focus and isn't reliably announced by all screen readers
why_it_matters: Placeholder-as-label is one of the most common real-world accessibility failures — the label vanishes exactly when the user starts interacting with the field

check: Icon-only buttons have an accessible name
description: A button containing only an icon (no visible text) has an aria-label attribute describing its action, e.g. Close or Delete item
why_it_matters: Without an accessible name, a screen reader announces an icon-only button as just 'button,' giving the user no information about what it does

check: Heading hierarchy is sequential with no skipped levels
description: Headings go h1 to h2 to h3 in document order without skipping a level for purely visual/styling reasons
why_it_matters: Screen reader users navigate by heading level to understand page structure — a skipped level breaks that mental model of the page's outline

check: Don't convey information by color alone
description: Any UI element using color to communicate meaning (error state, status, category) also uses an icon, text, or pattern — never color as the only signal
why_it_matters: Users with color vision deficiencies (a meaningful percentage of the population) can't reliably distinguish color-only signals

check: Respect prefers-reduced-motion
description: Animations and transitions are reduced or disabled when the user's OS-level reduced-motion setting is enabled
why_it_matters: Motion can trigger vestibular disorders/discomfort for some users — respecting this system preference is a low-effort, high-impact accommodation

check: Skip-to-content link for keyboard/screen reader users
description: A visually-hidden-until-focused 'Skip to main content' link as the first focusable element on the page
why_it_matters: Without it, a keyboard/screen reader user must tab through the entire navigation on every single page before reaching the actual content

check: Escape route from every modal/dialog
description: Every modal can be dismissed via the Escape key or a visible close button, and doesn't trap focus permanently inside it
why_it_matters: A modal with no dismiss path traps keyboard users entirely — this is a critical, complete-blocker-level accessibility failure, not a minor one

check: Touch targets at least 44x44px on mobile
description: Interactive elements on touch interfaces have a minimum tappable area of roughly 44x44px, even if the visual element is smaller
why_it_matters: Undersized touch targets cause mis-taps, disproportionately affecting users with motor impairments or larger fingers, on every touch-based device

check: Run an automated accessibility scan in CI
description: Integrate an automated a11y linter such as axe-core (via Playwright/Cypress) or an eslint accessibility plugin into the CI pipeline as a required check
why_it_matters: Automated tools catch roughly a third of WCAG issues reliably (contrast, missing labels, ARIA misuse) — free, fast, regression-preventing coverage that manual review alone won't consistently catch

check: Largest Contentful Paint under 2.5 seconds
description: The largest visible content element (usually a hero image or heading) renders within 2.5 seconds of navigation start, measured under real-world conditions (field data, not just lab)
why_it_matters: LCP is one of Google's three Core Web Vitals and directly affects both user-perceived load speed and search ranking

check: Interaction to Next Paint under 200 milliseconds
description: The delay between a user interaction (click, tap, key press) and the next visual update stays under 200ms across the session, not just on the first interaction
why_it_matters: INP replaced First Input Delay as a Core Web Vital — it measures actual interaction responsiveness throughout the page's lifetime, not just the first click

check: Cumulative Layout Shift under 0.1
description: Visible content doesn't unexpectedly shift position after initial render — reserve space for images, ads, embeds, and async-loaded content before it arrives
why_it_matters: Unexpected layout shift causes mis-clicks and a jarring experience, and is directly measured as a Core Web Vital affecting search ranking

check: Images served in modern formats with responsive sizing
description: Serve WebP or AVIF with a JPEG/PNG fallback where needed, and use responsive srcset sizing (or a framework's Image component) so mobile devices don't download desktop-sized images
why_it_matters: Unoptimized images are consistently the single largest contributor to page weight and slow LCP on real-world sites

check: Lazy-load below-the-fold images and heavy embeds
description: Set lazy loading (or a framework equivalent) on images and iframes not visible in the initial viewport
why_it_matters: Loading everything eagerly wastes bandwidth and delays the resources that actually matter for the initial paint

check: Code-split by route, not one monolithic bundle
description: Use route-level dynamic imports (Next.js does this automatically for pages; verify it for anything manually bundled) so users download only the JS the current page needs
why_it_matters: A single large bundle delays Time to Interactive for every page even when most of the code isn't used on the current one

check: Third-party scripts loaded async/defer, and audited for necessity
description: Analytics, chat widgets, ad scripts — load them non-blocking, and periodically audit whether each one is still actually needed
why_it_matters: Unmanaged third-party scripts are a common, often-overlooked source of both performance regression and security/privacy risk (each one is a third party with code execution on your page)

check: Font loading doesn't block text rendering
description: Web fonts use a swap or optional font-display setting so text renders in a fallback font immediately rather than staying invisible until the custom font loads
why_it_matters: The default font-display behavior can cause invisible text while a custom font loads on a slow connection — swap avoids blank text at the cost of a brief font swap flash

check: No render-blocking resources in the critical path unnecessarily
description: CSS/JS that isn't needed for above-the-fold content is deferred or loaded async rather than blocking initial render
why_it_matters: Every unnecessary render-blocking resource directly delays LCP and Time to Interactive

check: Bundle size monitored in CI with a budget/threshold
description: Set a size budget for the main JS bundle and fail CI (or at least warn loudly) if a PR pushes it meaningfully over — catch bundle bloat before it ships, not after a user complains
why_it_matters: Bundle size regressions accumulate silently one 'small' dependency addition at a time without an enforced budget to catch them

check: Measure with real field data, not just lab scores
description: Lighthouse/lab scores are useful for local iteration, but validate against real-user field data (Chrome UX Report, or your own RUM) since lab conditions don't reflect real device/network diversity
why_it_matters: A page can score perfectly in a lab test on a fast dev machine and still perform poorly for real users on mid-range mobile devices over cellular connections — field data is what Google's ranking systems and real users actually experience
